analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 6 finding(s) from 10 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: Debug mode bypasses reglk_ctrl in rng_wrapper, allowing writes to RNG polynomials even when locked. pkt_wrapper unconditionally allows writes to fuse request/address registers, circumventing the lock. This violates the expected lock-based write protection.
findings.vulnerability_category: write to protected configuration/control registers of peripherals (RSA, RNG, PKT, etc.) / registers holding keys, mode settings, or access-control policy / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: CPU bus master (or debug master) writing to peripheral registers Operation: write to protected configuration/control registers of peripherals (RSA, RNG, PKT, etc.) Object: registers holding keys, mode settings, or access-control policy Expected guard: reglk_ctrl[peripheral_index] == 1 blocks writes; set-once or sticky, not clearable except by a secure reset Observed behavior: In rng_wrapper.sv, when debug_mode_i is asserted, writes to security-critical registers (poly128, poly64, poly32, poly16) bypass the reglk_ctrl gate (e.g., `poly128[3] <= debug_mode_i ? wdata[31:0] : (reglk_ctrl_i[5] ? poly128[3] : wdata[31:0]);`). In pkt_wrapper.sv, writes to fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o completely ignore reglk_ctrl. In rsa_wrapper.sv, some writes use reglk_ctrl but do not check debug_mode_i. This creates a path where debug mode or unlocked writes can modify protected registers even when lock bits are set. Path: AXI write -> rng_wrapper (debug_mode_i bypass) / pkt_wrapper (no lock check) / rsa_wrapper (no debug guard) Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Debug mode can overwrite RNG polynomial configuration and may trigger fuse reads without lock consent, potentially exposing secret state. The lock mechanism is not consistently enforced, violating the expected guard.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact behavior of reglk_ctrl reset and whether lock bits are writable from non-secure contexts is not fully visible. The fuse address write in pkt_wrapper may be benign if subsequent reads are gated (see OB-003).

findings.summary: Debug mode exposes internal RNG state (seeds, rand_seg, cs_state) regardless of lock bits. This violates the security requirement and must be fixed.
findings.vulnerability_category: read or modify internal RNG state (entropy, seeds, control registers) through bus interface / RNG internal registers and output (rand_num_o, seed128_o, etc.) / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: debug software executing in debug mode, or debug hardware access Operation: read or modify internal RNG state (entropy, seeds, control registers) through bus interface Object: RNG internal registers and output (rand_num_o, seed128_o, etc.) Expected guard: debug_mode_i must disable read access to live random numbers or seeds, or a lock bit must prevent any access when debug is active; alternatively, RNG should be inaccessible in debug mode Observed behavior: In rng_wrapper.sv, the read path explicitly allows debug_mode to bypass the lock and read internal seed values and random segments. For example: `rdata = debug_mode_i ? {16'b0, seed16_big_o} : (reglk_ctrl_i[7] ? 0 : {16'b0, seed16_big_o});`. Similar bypasses exist for other read addresses (23,24,25). This means when debug_mode is active, the RNG internal state (seeds, rand_seg, cs_state) can be read regardless of the lock bit. Path: debug_mode_i -> rng_wrapper.sv read case -> direct read of seed_big_o, seed64_big_o, rand_seg, cs_state Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Debug mode can expose the complete internal state of the RNG, compromising future random numbers and potentially enabling prediction of secret keys derived from RNG. This is a serious violation of the expected guard.

findings.summary: Bootrom selection is not locked and bootrom remains accessible via the NoC, violating the expected guard that bootrom should be inaccessible after boot.
findings.vulnerability_category: read bootrom contents or execute code from bootrom / bootrom memory (i_bootrom_bm, i_bootrom_linux) / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: any bus master (CPU, debug) accessing bootrom Operation: read bootrom contents or execute code from bootrom Object: bootrom memory (i_bootrom_bm, i_bootrom_linux) Expected guard: bootrom should be read-only and inaccessible after initial boot, or boot_sel must be locked/sticky after boot; debug must not be able to change boot_sel or read bootrom Observed behavior: The ariane_boot_sel_i signal selects between two bootrom instances. There is no lock or gating mechanism to prevent runtime changes to this selection. The bootrom is accessible via the NoC (buf_ariane_bootrom_noc2_*), meaning any bus master that can reach that address range can read the bootrom. No post-boot disable is visible. Path: ariane_boot_sel_i -> mux for rom_req_acct -> bootrom instance -> rom_rdata; accessible via bootrom NoC interface Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An attacker with bus access can read the bootrom content at runtime, potentially extracting secure boot code. The selection between baremetal and Linux bootroms could be changed to influence execution flow.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Whether the bootrom address space is locked by a PMP or if the core's fetch logic inhibits access after boot is not visible in the provided snippets. The lack of an explicit lock in the peripheral file suggests it may be unprotected.

findings.summary: Insufficient evidence to confirm or refute the obligation.
findings.vulnerability_category: write to protected CSRs (e.g., mstatus, mcontrol) that should be locked / CSRs related to security, debug, or privilege / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: hart-specific write flags (we_flag_0..4) Operation: write to protected CSRs (e.g., mstatus, mcontrol) that should be locked Object: CSRs related to security, debug, or privilege Expected guard: we_flag must be set appropriately (e.g., only by debug or machine mode) and must not be writable by untrusted code; after lock, we_flag should be sticky Observed behavior: we_flag_[0-4] are inputs to riscv_peripherals; we_flag_4 is connected to dmi_jtag's we_flag port. No further usage or CSR write logic involving these flags is visible in the provided tool observations. The internal handling within dmi_jtag or CSR modules is unknown. Path: we_flag_* -> dmi_jtag (we_flag_4) -> unknown Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: Unable to determine if we_flags enforce write protection on CSRs.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The internal logic of dmi_jtag and the CSR write control path is not available. Without it, the effectiveness of we_flags cannot be assessed.

findings.summary: The multiple independent reset domains allow partial resets that can leave security state inconsistent, violating the expected guard that lock bits and key registers are only cleared by a power-on reset.
findings.vulnerability_category: reset or clear of security state registers (lock bits, key registers) through test/reset signals / reglk_ctrl bits, acc_ctrl bits, key registers, RNG state, RSA keys / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: test mode or selective resets Operation: reset or clear of security state registers (lock bits, key registers) through test/reset signals Object: reglk_ctrl bits, acc_ctrl bits, key registers, RNG state, RSA keys Expected guard: testmode_i must not affect security locks; resets should clear sensitive state; lock bits must not be cleared by any reset except power-on reset Observed behavior: The design uses separate reset signals (rst_1..rst_13) for different peripherals. For example, rng_wrapper uses `rst_ni && ~rst_10`, rsa_wrapper uses `rst_ni && ~rst_13`. This allows selective reset of peripheral logic without affecting the global rst_ni. The reglk_ctrl and acc_ctrl registers are likely in a wrapper (reglk_wrapper) tied to rst_9, allowing them to be reset independently. testmode_i is passed to dmi_jtag but its effect on locks is not visible. The partial reset architecture may leave lock bits intact while clearing key registers, or vice versa, violating the requirement that sensitive state must be uniformly cleared. Path: rst_* signals distributed to individual wrappers; reglk_wrapper uses rst_9; RSA uses rst_13; RNG uses rst_10; PKT uses rst_5 Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An attacker or fault could reset key registers while leaving lock bits set, or reset lock bits while keys remain, compromising security. The expected guard that all sensitive state is cleared only by power-on reset is not met.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The generation logic for rst_* signals and the exact effect of testmode_i on lock registers are not visible. Whether the reglk_ctrl bits are reset by rst_9 only or by other means is unknown.

findings.summary: CLINT access is not gated by privilege level, violating the expected guard. Any bus master can write to CLINT registers.
findings.vulnerability_category: write to CLINT registers (mtimer, mtimecmp, msip) / CLINT memory-mapped registers / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: CLINT bus interface Operation: write to CLINT registers (mtimer, mtimecmp, msip) Object: CLINT memory-mapped registers Expected guard: access should be limited to machine mode (or as specified) and should not be accessible via debug unless allowed; address decoding must prevent aliasing Observed behavior: The CLINT module is connected via a noc_axilite_bridge that translates NoC requests directly into AXI-lite transactions for the CLINT. There is no privilege check or acc_ctrl gating in this path. The clint_axi_req signals are driven directly from the NoC bridge. No firewall is present. Path: NoC -> noc_axilite_bridge for CLINT -> clint_axi_req -> CLINT module Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Any bus master with access to the CLINT address range can modify timers and IPIs, potentially disrupting real-time behavior or causing denial-of-service. Debug mode could also access CLINT if not filtered.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The overall address map routing may limit which masters can reach the CLINT NoC port, but no on-chip guards are visible in the provided RTL.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
